changeReason: Improved translation from Japanese. Old: "What's in the Box?"

TextENGNew: Do you believe that life offers you choices?
There are many people who lament the following:
If only there were clear-cut choices at life's turning points, I could weigh them carefully and guide myself toward a better future.
...Every time I overhear such laments, I think what a trivial worry that is.
Even if you were given clear choices, they'd be meaningless in the end -- none of them would lead you to a better future.
...Is this hard to understand?
Then let me place two mysterious boxes before you.
In other words, you have two clear choices.
Do you open the red box, or the blue box?
A lot of things would be uncertain even with that choice, wouldn't they?
If you must open one, then your natural impulse would be to choose whichever holds the better outcome for you.
And after examining each box's shape and feel, weighing various factors, you would eventually -- after much agonizing -- choose either the red or the blue.
...If this was you, which box would you open?
Red or blue?
...If you were to go by their traditional meanings, then red would be a dangerous, threatening color.
However, that doesn't automatically mean that it would be calm and relaxing inside the blue box, either.
In fact, the colors themselves might be a trap, designed to make you wary of red so you'd choose blue.
A trap?
...Could it be the contents of the box aren't a reward, but rather a penalty?
See...? Now you're at a loss.
Torn between red and blue, you've surely begun wishing for the option of walking away without opening either.
But there isn't.@k You have to open either the red box or the blue box.
Oh, I forgot to mention -- when you choose one box, the other vanishes.
So you'll never learn what was inside the one you didn't choose.@kJust adding that last rule for you.
Now.@k Why don't you choose?
The red box, or the blue box?
...Don't worry, neither one contains anything bad.@k...Go on.
changeReason: Original says 'you won't lose anything by picking either one' while new says 'neither contains anything bad' — the Japanese (損なものは入っていない) refers to the contents not being harmful, not about the act of choosing.

TextENGNew: Have you thought about it?
In the end, you chose this color, didn't you?
...As soon as you chose, the other box disappeared.
So you can give up on the contents of that one, okay?
That's the rule.
Now, open up the box you chose.
Inside the box there was... a piece of caramel.
Inside the box there was... a stick of chewing gum.
...I know you're a little disappointed.
Well that's only natural.
No matter how you look at it, it looks like you drew the dud.
For all you know, the right box might have had a chocolate bar in it.
No, in fact, something incredible like a pair of tickets to Hawaii might have been in there.
But even if you wanted to verify that, the other box has already disappeared.
There's no way you can check now.
But if you think about it from an optimistic point of view...
Just maybe... the other box was empty, and this box was the winner.
And so, satisfied (or perhaps not) with your cheap prize, you toss it into your mouth and chew away contentedly.
So, what do you think in the end?
If you were given a second chance, would you try to open the other box?
...But I'm sorry to say, the game of choosing between the red and blue box will never come to you again.
The chance to change your selection will never come.
Don't your parents always say you only get one chance at life's choices, so choose carefully?
Hee hee hee...
See?@kChoices aren't all they're cracked up to be.@k...A little disillusioned?@kAhahahahahaha...
...Right now you're thinking: ``That's it?``
That's right, the contents of the red box and the blue box were a piece of caramel and a stick of chewing gum.
You might have thought you drew the short straw earlier, but seeing them side by side like this, you can't really say either one was worse.
Well, I guess everybody has their personal preferences.
If you prefer caramel to gum, for example.
...Based on your personal taste, you're undoubtedly thinking you'd like to choose again.
...The kind of choice you've been longing for is essentially this:
The selfish desire to peek inside both boxes, compare their contents, and pick the better one.
But you see,@k reality is the same as this game.
If you pick one, the one you didn't choose disappears.@k So there's no way you could check the result.
If only, at that time,@kI had done this,@kor perhaps hadn't done that...@kI could surely be happier (or perhaps unhappier) than I am now -- but there's simply no way to know.
changeReason: Original says 'it wouldn't be hard to imagine' (speculative) while the Japanese (わかりようもない) means 'there's no way to know' — the new translation captures the intended futility rather than speculation.

TextENGNew: In the end, you only have the pleasure of approving of or being disappointed by the choice that you did make.
But that's fine.
After all, didn't you enjoy the thrill of making the choice?
Now that you know the contents of both boxes,@kthe choice between red and blue wouldn't even serve as a diversion.

Because rather than playing this tedious box game, you could be gazing up at the mercurial summer evening sky, listening for distant thunder, and wondering whether the rain will come -- and that would be far more enjoyable.
